nativeRange.habitat: Sandy reef flats, seagrass beds, Halimeda-dominated sand communities, and shallow backreef zones. Anchors into soft sandy or rubble substrate via a large bulbous rhizoidal holdfast. Common in calm, sunlit, shallow tropical water across the Caribbean and western Atlantic.

decorPreferences: deep sandy substrate (at least 10 cm) for bulbous rhizoidal holdfast burial
open display placement with strong overhead lighting
low to moderate flow zone to prevent uprooting
stable positioning away from heavy herbivore grazing pressure

avoidWith: urchins that may dislodge or consume the holdfast
large herbivorous fish such as tangs that may uproot or graze calcareous algae
strong powerhead flow directed at the specimen

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product image from Mosaic Macros listing for Pinecone Macroalgae (Rhipocephalus phoenix). Sole known hobby-trade source for this specialty entry.

summary: A heavily calcified calcareous green macroalga with a distinctive pinecone- or Christmas-tree-shaped thallus of nested whorled blades, anchored in Caribbean sand via a bulbous rhizoidal holdfast. Considered notoriously difficult to maintain long-term in captivity; generally suited to experienced reef keepers.
careNotes:
Rhipocephalus phoenix (Pinecone Algae, also known as Christmas Tree Algae) is a calcareous green macroalga in the family Halimedaceae (formerly often placed in Udoteaceae), order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae — closely related to Halimeda and the Penicillus/Udotea group. Note: some older sources (including the original task brief) list the family as Udoteaceae; current molecular taxonomy broadly places Rhipocephalus within Halimedaceae, confirmed by GBIF, EOL, and reefguide.org. The thallus consists of a series of nested whorls of flattened, calcified blades arranged around a central axis, giving a pinecone- or Christmas-tree appearance. The structure grows upright from sandy substrate, anchored by a large bulbous holdfast containing the rhizoids. Specimens in the hobby trade are typically 2–3 inches (5–7.5 cm) tall at sale; mature plants in the wild are documented to reach 6 inches (15 cm) and 2–3 inches (5–7.5 cm) in diameter.

SHORT-LIVED / CAPTIVE DIFFICULTY WARNING: At least one well-regarded marine reference database (Reeflex) states that there are no reports of this species being successfully maintained in captivity, placing it among the most challenging calcareous green macroalgae alongside Udotea and Acetabularia. Mosaic Macros is the sole hobby trade source found for this species at time of research (2026-06-08). The retail-grade source marineplantbook.com rates difficulty as 'Easy,' which is considered inconsistent with the broader body of evidence and likely reflects optimistic retail bias; the advanced rating applied here is based on the reeflex caveat and the parallel captive difficulty documented for sibling genera (Penicillus, Udotea). Hobbyists should treat specimen decline within weeks to months as a likely outcome even in well-maintained systems.

CALCIUM AND ALKALINITY: As a strongly calcareous alga depositing aragonite (CaCO3) into its blade structure, R. phoenix is generally advised to be kept with stable reef-grade calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1,250–1,350 ppm), and alkalinity (8–12 dKH). Instability in these parameters is reported to accelerate thallus decline across related Halimedaceae.

SAND DEPTH: A deep sand bed is considered necessary for rhizoidal holdfast establishment — marineplantbook specifies 4 inches (approximately 10 cm) or more. Without adequate substrate depth the thallus will not anchor and typically tips over and fails. The sand should be left relatively undisturbed around the holdfast base.

LIGHTING: Moderate to high lighting is generally advised, consistent with shallow tropical reef conditions. Calcareous green macroalgae in this family tend to bleach and decline under low-light conditions.

FLOW: Low to moderate flow is preferred — sufficient for gas exchange but gentle enough not to dislodge the sandy substrate anchor or stress the thallus.

PALATABILITY: The heavily calcified thallus is considered largely unpalatable to most marine fish and invertebrates, giving it reasonable grazing resistance in a reef community context. Urchins and large herbivorous tangs may still uproot or damage specimens.

TAXONOMY NOTE: The family placement of Rhipocephalus has varied in the literature. Older (pre-molecular) sources assign it to Udoteaceae alongside Udotea and Penicillus. Current molecular phylogenies (as reflected in GBIF, EOL, and reefguide.org) more commonly place it in Halimedaceae alongside Halimeda. Both circumscriptions refer to the same order (Bryopsidales); the distinction is at family rank only.

SIBLING GENERA: See also Mermaid's Tail (Udotea sp., sw-algae-058), Merman's Shaving Brush (Penicillus dumetosus, sw-algae-060), and Pencil Top (Penicillus capitatus, sw-algae-069) — all members of the same order (Bryopsidales) with a shared short-lived captive reputation, sand-substrate anchoring requirement, and calcium/alkalinity sensitivity.

SOURCE COVERAGE CAVEAT: This entry is based on the Mosaic Macros product page (sole hobby-trade source found), supplemented by marineplantbook.com, reeflex.net, and reefguide.org. Species-specific aquarium husbandry literature is sparse. Water parameters and care values reflect standard tropical reef chemistry and are consistent with related genera in this database. Mosaic Macros is documented as the primary hobby source.
breedingNotes: Propagation by fragmentation has been reported in hobby literature (marineplantbook lists it as a method alongside sporulation), though success rates in captivity are not well documented given the overall difficulty of maintaining this species long-term. Sexual reproduction via the gametophyte-sporophyte alternation typical of green algae is not a practical captive propagation method. Most aquarium specimens are collected rather than aquaculture-propagated.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby-trade source found for this specialty entry. Product listing confirms scientific name Rhipocephalus phoenix, common name 'Pinecone Macroalgae', heavily calcified, grows up from sandbed, unpalatable to most marine fish and invertebrates. Size sold: 2–3 inch plant. Minimal husbandry detail on page. Product image URL: https://mosaicmacros.com/cdn/shop/files/IMG-3964.jpg

additional.name: marineplantbook.com — Rhipocephalus phoenix
additional.notes: Provided morphology (pinecone/Christmas-tree shaped, 6" tall x 2–3" diameter), substrate requirement (4" deep sand bed), temperature range (76–86°F / 24–30°C), lighting (moderate to high), flow (moderate), propagation methods (fragmentation and sporulation), palatability (not palatable), and habitat (reef flats with sandy substrate, seagrass beds, Halimeda forests). Difficulty listed as 'Easy' — considered an optimistic retail rating inconsistent with reeflex captive-record caveat; advanced rating applied instead.

additional.name: Reeflex — Rhipocephalus phoenix
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy: family Halimedaceae, order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae. Size range 6–12 cm. Depth range 1–45 m. Distribution: Belize, Colombia, Cuba, Florida, Gulf of Mexico, Haiti, Jamaica, Mexico, Panama, Puerto Rico, Bahamas. Critically notes: 'There are no reports available yet that this animal has already been kept in captivity successfully.' Used to justify 'advanced' care level over retail 'Easy' rating.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org — Pinecone Alga (Caribbean)
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed taxonomy (family Halimedaceae, order Bryopsidales), size 6–12 cm, depth 1–45 m, distribution Caribbean, Bahamas, South Florida. Photographic records from Nassau, Exumas (Bahamas), Key West (Florida).

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
